attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with vaginal bleeding for three weeks. She reached menopause two years ago. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her BMI is 25 kg/m2. Her parent died of colorectal cancer at age 45, and her sister was diagnosed with colorectal cancer at age 42. Her maternal aunt died of endometrial cancer at age 50.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. Speculum examination reveals a small amount of blood in the vaginal vault. Transvaginal ultrasound shows endometrial thickness of 15 mm (>4 mm). Endometrial pipelle sampling reveals endometrioid adenocarcinoma.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying genetic condition?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with post-menopausal bleeding and is found to have endometrial cancer. The family history strongly suggests Lynch syndrome, an autosomal dominant condition that predisposes to multiple cancers, particularly colorectal and endometrial cancer. The early age of onset of colorectal cancer in multiple family members (parent at 45, sister at 42) and endometrial cancer in a relative (aunt at 50) are classic features of Lynch syndrome.

BRCA1 mutation carriers have an increased risk of breast and ovarian cancer, but not typically colorectal or endometrial cancer. While endometrial cancer can occur, the pattern of family cancers in this case is not typical for BRCA1.

Li-Fraumeni syndrome is characterised by early-onset breast cancer, soft tissue sarcomas, brain tumours, and adrenocortical carcinomas. While it can include colorectal cancer, the pattern of cancers in this family is not typical.

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome is associated with hamartomatous polyps, mucocutaneous pigmentation, and increased risk of various cancers including colorectal cancer. However, the lack of characteristic pigmentation and the strong endometrial cancer history make this less likely.

Cowden syndrome is characterised by multiple hamartomas and increased risk of breast, thyroid, and endometrial cancer. The strong colorectal cancer history in this family makes this diagnosis less likely.

Women with Lynch syndrome have a 40-60% lifetime risk of endometrial cancer and should be offered risk-reducing surgery after completing their family. Genetic counselling and testing should be arranged to confirm the diagnosis and guide surveillance for other Lynch syndrome-associated cancers.
learningPoints: Post-menopausal bleeding requires urgent investigation due to high risk of endometrial cancer.
Lynch syndrome should be suspected in patients with endometrial cancer and strong family history of colorectal cancer.
Endometrial thickness >4mm on transvaginal ultrasound in post-menopausal women requires further investigation.
Lynch syndrome carries a 40-60% lifetime risk of endometrial cancer.
references: Zhao S, Chen L, Zang Y, Liu W, Liu S, Teng F, Xue F, Wang Y. Endometrial cancer in Lynch syndrome. Int J Cancer. 2022 Jan 1;150(1):7-17. doi: 10.1002/ijc.33763. Epub 2021 Sep 9. PMID: 34398969.
